texts.orig: Industry is responsible for approximatively one-third of global fi nal energy use and around 40% of total energy-related CO2 emissions. Despite continuous e ffi ciency increase in energy utilization, overall growing industrial production has led the CO2 emissions to rise [1]. This is particularly true for the iron and steel industry, where the production increase in the past two decades is closely related to the development of emergent economies such as China and India. Global steel production almost doubled between 2000 and 2014, going from 0.85 MTons/year to 1.67 MTons/year [2]. GHG emissions depend on the production technology. Two production technologies account for most of the steel produced globally: Integrated steel mills based on the Blast Furnace -Blast Oxygen Furnace (BF-BOF), and the Electric Arc Furnace based on recycled steel or scrap. texts.label: text
texts.orig: New programs seeking to improve energy e ffi ciency and reduce GHG emissions from the steelmaking industry are studying alternative uses for blast furnace gas such as the VALORCO project in France. One promising alternative is CO2 and CO utilization in which emissions are used for the production of valuable products, like industrial intermediates and fuels synthetized from CO or CO2 or both. A gas separation and concentration technology is necessary to recover industry emissions and produce gas stream(s) suitable for carbon utilization technologies. This paper describes the application of commercially available polymeric gas separation membranes to blast furnace gas. Data for two commercially available membranes, a H2 selective glassy polyimide membrane from UBE industries and a CO2 selective rubbery composite membrane from MTR Inc. along with a process economic modelling have been used for the calculation of product composition paths and associated costs depending on product purity and recovery. Relatively low separation costs, from 15 to 33 EUR/ Ton separated CO2, seem promising for the use of membrane technology as a concentration tuning box prior to carbon utilization technologies, notably for mid to high CO2 purity requirements, and allow to simultaneously achieve CO2 capture and CO recovery.

texts.orig: Blast furnace gas (BFG) emissions are the most important in volume and represent around 69% of the emitted CO2. It is typically burned for heating and power generation often being mixed with natural gas, coke oven gas or blast oxygen furnace gas to increase its heating value.

texts.orig: Technologies typically applied for CO2 and CO separation share common characteristics in their operation, the main di ff erence being the separation agent itself and the nature of its interactions with the gas species to be separated. These separation processes include chemical absorption by amine systems in the case of CO2 retention, and by copper salt solutions for CO; physical absorption for CO2 by selexol or Rectisol processes; adsorption processes like pressure swing adsorption (PSA) or vacuum pressure swing adsorption (VPSA) over CO2 and/ or CO selective adsorbents; cryogenic separation, and membrane separation. To our knowledge, only two recent research programs have proposed separation technologies for the speci fi c case of the iron and steel industry and have carried out tests on pilot level installations, both within CO2 capture and storage scenarios. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas separation has been proposed as a potential technology for CO2 separation from fl ue gases with CO2 contents lower than those present in BFG, and it is now a commercial alternative for separation of CO2 in the natural and biogas industry as well as for CO and H2 puri fi cation in syngas applications. Part of this is associated to the many advantages o ff ered by this technology over more classical processes such as reduced environmental impact due to the absence of chemical systems, easy up and down scaling, ease of operation and control, no moving parts, no additional energy required besides the one required for gas fl ow, among others. However, very few works have been published dealing with the application of membrane separation to steelmaking o ff -gases. This study addresses the question of the application of commercially available gas separation membranes to BFG gas separation for CO2 and CO utilization and aims to illustrate current opportunities and challenges. The overall framework, sketched on Fig. 2, is signi fi cantly di ff erent from the post-combustion carbon capture and sequestration (CCS) situation, which has been investigated in numerous studies up to now [10 -13]; the objective of the membrane separation box is indeed to o ff er, from the quaternary feed mixture (CO2, CO, N2, H2) a slight modi fi cation of the gas composition of the retentate and permeate streams, in order to facilitate one of the utilization possibilities. A very high CO2 recovery and purity goal, both typically ≥ 90% for CCS applications, is not required. Rather, a bulk removal of one species (e.g. CO2), associated to a concentration increase of another (e.g. CO, H2), might be of interest in a technical economic scenario.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More speci fi cally, data for two representative and commercially available membranes, a glassy, H2 selective polyimide membrane from UBE industries and a rubbery, CO2 selective composite membrane from MTR Inc. have been used for the simulation of blast furnace gas separation in a membrane process by means of an in-house developed gas permeation simulation tool. The e ff ect of operating parameters (pressure ratio, membrane surface, stage cut) on separation performances was studied and a general mapping of gas compositions attainable with the available commercial membranes is presented and discussed under a gas utilization scenario. Energy and surface requirements are taken into consideration and separation costs are expressed per ton of recovered CO2. Based on a systematic parametric sensitivity analysis, a set of operating conditions has been identi fi ed with overall separation costs as low as 25 € /ton of CO2 for a two-stage separation with 95% CO2 recovery. Additionally a concentrated CO stream can be recovered on the reject (high pressure) side. The interest of this promising performances for carbon capture and utilization is discussed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The design basis is 550,000 Nm 3 /h of blast furnace gas at 1.05 bar and 35 °C with the composition presented in Table 1. This volume corresponds to the sum of gas currently being burned for power generation and fl aring in a french site producing around 6.6 million tons of steel per year. It represents roughly 50% of total BFG produced on site. CO2 and CO mole fraction are around 22% and 23% respectively. For comparison purposes with other industrial emissions, this CO2 fraction is around the same as for cement kiln fl ue gas and it is almost the double of that of a pulverized coal fi red power plant fl ue gas [14]. CO fraction is close to that of gases exiting the steam reforming of natural gas before the Water gas shift or puri fi cation stages [15]. BFG is considered water saturated and free of impurities such as particles, NOX, SOX and H2S.

texts.text: 2.2. Membrane selection

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite the high number of polymers available for gas separation, only a limited number has made it to commercial production. Most common polymers used in commercial gas separation modules include cellulose acetate, polysulfones, polycarbonates, polyimides, per fl uoropolymers and polydimethylsiloxanes [16,17]. Permeability/permeance data of commercial membranes is often not available in the literature, and when it is, it is often limited to the gas couple for which the membrane is mainly commercialized. This makes comparing all available membranes for a given application a di ffi cult task, particularly if the gas to be treated is a multicomponent mixture, as it is the case of the blast furnace gas. For a gas utilization scenario based on CO2, a CO2 selective membrane is of interest in order to reach a stream with the highest level of purity and free of inert species such as N2 and Ar. Recently, a highly permeable membrane has been developed by MTR under the name Polaris ® . Permeances for the gas species present in the BFG have been published in works dealing with CO2 capture in
texts.text: Despite the high number of polymers available for gas separation, only a limited number has made it to commercial production. Most common polymers used in commercial gas separation modules include cellulose acetate, polysulfones, polycarbonates, polyimides, per fl uoropolymers and polydimethylsiloxanes [16,17]. Permeability/permeance data of commercial membranes is often not available in the literature, and when it is, it is often limited to the gas couple for which the membrane is mainly commercialized. This makes comparing all available membranes for a given application a di ffi cult task, particularly if the gas to be treated is a multicomponent mixture, as it is the case of the blast furnace gas. For a gas utilization scenario based on CO2, a CO2 selective membrane is of interest in order to reach a stream with the highest level of purity and free of inert species such as N2 and Ar. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Process simulations were carried out in Aspen Plus ® software, version 8.6. Peng-Robinson fl uid package available in the software was used for energy calculations. Compressors and vacuum pumps are considered to be isentropic. Isentropic e ffi ciency is taken as 0.8 and mechanical e ffi ciency is taken as 0.9. Multi-stage operation with interstage cooling was calculated both for the feed compression and permeate vacuum power use under the following recommended constraints: maximum stage outlet gas temperature 175 °C, and equal compression ratios for each stage. Resulting compression ratios per stage were ~3 or less. Either two or three compression stages were needed depending on the pressure requirements. Shell and tube heat exchangers for gas cooling after each stage were calculated in countercurrent fl ow with a mean constant value for the heat transfer coe ffi cient (U) of 105 W/m 2 K corresponding to the case of pressurized gases on the shell side and cooling water on the tube side [21]. Cooling water inlet and outlet temperature were taken as 24 °C and 34 °C respectively. Gas outlet temperature was taken as 35 °C.

texts.orig: 2.4. Economic calculations
texts.text: 2.4. Economic calculations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Cost calculations were done by implementing the cost model presented in Table 3 within the Aspen Plus ® simulations using executable Fortran statements. Cost parameters chosen are presented on Table 4. Cost model and cost model parameters used here are based on those reported in literature published in membrane process design for carbon capture applications [10 -13]. Capital cost includes membrane area and membrane module skids, compression, vacuum pumps and heat exchangers. Membrane surface investment was estimated using a membrane cost of 40 EUR/m 2 . Membrane frame investment was calculated using the cost equation originally proposed by Van der Sluijs et al. [25] and modi fi ed by Roussanaly et al.[10] to take into account the e ff ect of operating pressure on the projected costs. The minimum pressure considered for the membrane frame cost equation was 5 bar either for feed compression or permeate vacuum. Compressor cost was calculated supposing a centrifugal compressor and using the regressed equation from an estimating chart proposed in the classical literature of process economics [26]. A material factor of 2.5 for stainless steel is included. The equation predicts costs per kW in the range of common fi xed values used in most studies (~1000 EUR/ kW) for the compression range considered in this study. The vacuum pump investment is calculated using a fi xed cost of 1500 EUR/kW based of the value of 1000 USD/kW reported by Zhai et al. [18] and adjusting for construction in stainless steel according to a factor of 2.0/ 1.3 related to carbon steel [26]. Shell and tube heat exchangers cost is calculated using a regressed equation from [26]. Stainless steel material factor and pressure factor for 40 bar are included in the cost estimation.

In a feed compression operation, because CO conversion processes usually require high pressure conditions, the possibility to simultaneously provide a bulk removal for CO2 and the production of a concentrated pressurized CO stream at the retentate is of major interest. This situation largely di ff ers from the post-combustion capture from fl ue gases, where CO2 removal generates a N2 rich stream with non-economic interest. texts.orig: The minimum in the cost curves correlates to the in fl exion point of the tradeo ff curves, for which a higher purity is reached at the expense of a pronounced lost in recovery. In such a region, the same purity can be obtained with a higher recovery by increasing the pressure ratio. The higher product recovery compensates for the higher cost related to compression energy and capital cost, and the overall separation cost is lower in terms of EUR/ton CO2. For CO2 purities below the minimum, it is more economical to operate at lower pressures as long as there is no much di ff erence in recovery. This is exempli fi ed by the cost curve for the UBE membrane at a feed pressure of 5 bar. For purities below 40% CO2, separation cost is actually lower at higher pressure. This is also explained by the di ff erences in product recovery with pressure ratio.

texts.orig: The applied economic model is based on cost parameters typically used in CO2 capture research programs. For a particular project these parameters can vary both geographically and chronologically as well as be signi fi cantly di ff erent under speci fi c project circumstances. It is of interest to evaluate the e ff ect of variations of these parameters on the fi nal separation cost. Fig. 10 presents a sensitivity analysis of the speci fi c separation cost for a process looking to achieve a product stream with 70% CO2 content and a CO2 recovery of 85%. Results are given for both a permeate vacuum and a feed compression operation. As predicted from the cost breakdown, the separation cost under vacuum is most sensitive to membrane life and membrane cost which cause the largest variations. Energy cost impact is similar to the membrane cost and re fl ects the importance of the energy expenses, even in the vacuum operation case. Less pronounced variations are attributed to the capital cost of the vacuum pump and heat exchangers as well as cooling water and O & M cost factors. Membrane frame cost has little impact on separation cost, explained by its little share of capital costs. For the feed compression case, it is the electricity cost that is responsible for the largest variations in cost. Energy cost, and hence power needs, deserve special care during cost calculations, especially for compression scenarios. Equipment and operation cost parameters such as compressor cost, cooling water cost, O&M cost, and heat exchanger cost have a more important impact on separation cost than membrane parameters such as membrane lifetime and membrane cost.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the most interesting utilization pathways for CO and CO2 is the production of valuable chemical products such as methanol, urea, and methane among others species by means of catalytic conversion processes [27 -30]. Both product streams from the BFG membrane separation, a CO-rich retentate and a CO2-rich permeate could be potential candidates to be used as feedstock for such transformation processes. Table 5 lists the main reactions that take place during the production of chemicals from CO and CO2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the limitations of feed streams for catalytic processes is the presence of impurities along with the reactants. Depending on the nature of the impurities these can be toxic for the catalysts, such as oxygen in the case of CO2 reduction catalysts, or they can be inert components that reduce the concentration/partial pressure of reactants resulting in increase of reaction volumes and loss of reactants through inert purge streams. The main inert component present in both product streams from a one stage membrane separation is N2, especially in the CO-rich retentate for which N2 content is between 55 -75%. As explained before, dense polymeric membranes are not suited for CO-N2 separations, and so another technology must be used if further CO enrichment is needed.

texts.orig: On the other hand, N2 content in the permeate side can reach values under 10% but it does so at very low stage cuts/CO2 recoveries. However, permeate N2 content can be further reduced while keeping high recoveries by a multistage membrane separation as it has been proposed for CO2 capture scenarios [10,12]. Such a process is presented in Fig. 11, considering a two-stage permeate enricher con fi guration within the utilization perspective discussed above. Driving force choice depends on the utilization technologies to be coupled with the membrane separation. Feed compression would allow the production of a pressurized CO rich stream, which could be of interest depending on the utilization process conditions and if optional separation steps are needed and could take advantage of the high pressure. Another possibility is the compression of the product stream after separation instead of the feed stream, which would lead to lower energy requirements because a smaller volume is compressed, and product pressure could be adjusted independently of the membrane separation step. Driving force is created here by exerting a vacuum on the permeate side. As showed in Fig. 8, for moderate CO2 recoveries, vacuum operation o ff ers the lowest CO2 separation costs.

texts.orig: Optimization of process variables is needed in order to identify operation zones with the minimum cost that ful fi ll separation requirements given by the selected utilization pathway. One of the advantages of the developed tool is that it allows the use of the built-in optimization tools within the process software. Membrane surface for the two stages has been optimized with the objective of minimizing CO2 separation cost of the two stage enricher. As before, pressure ratio is kept constant and equal for both stages to reduce the complexity of the analysis. The resulting optimal separation cost for varying product constraints is presented in Table 6. A maximum N2 content of 5% was imposed as constraint for increasing recovery ratios of CO2 from 90% to 99% for the fi rst three cases. A more restrictive N2 content of 1% was imposed for the fourth case along with a 90% CO2 recovery. CO2 recoveries higher than 91% for the 1% N2 content constraint were not feasible under the simulated conditions. The increase in the CO2 recovery constraint has a notable e ff ect on the optimal separation cost and product composition. For the case with 99% recovery, N2 content reaches the 5% constraint, and higher recoveries would therefore not respect the 5% constraint. The 99% CO2 case has the highest cost with around 33.2 EUR/Ton CO2. Comparison between the fi rst and fourth cases shows that a reduction of N2 content can be reached at 90% CO2 recovery with a slight increase of costs resulting from an increase in total membrane area. This could be interesting for utilization paths that are very sensitive to the presence of inert species on the feed. CO2 purity is around 91% for both cases at 90% recovery. It lowers to 87% and 81% for the cases at 95% and 99% recovery respectively.

texts.orig: 4. Conclusions and outlook
texts.text: 4. Conclusions and outlook

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study intended to address the problem of blast furnace fl ue gas separation for carbon utilization purposes. The possibilities and limitations of two di ff erent (i.e. one glassy and one rubbery) commercially available membranes have been investigated through a parametric and technical-economic simulation. The situation largely di ff ers from post-combustion carbon capture framework in that a complex feed mixture has to be treated (CO2, CO, N2, H2) and a CO rich stream, o ff ering an added value, can be generated simultaneously to CO2 removal. The di ff erent composition ranges of a single stage and a two stage membrane process and the associated costs have been evaluated.
texts.text: This study intended to address the problem of blast furnace fl ue gas separation for carbon utilization purposes. The possibilities and limitations of two di ff erent (i.e. one glassy and one rubbery) commercially available membranes have been investigated through a parametric and technical-economic simulation. The situation largely di ff ers from post-combustion carbon capture framework in that a complex feed mixture has to be treated (CO2, CO, N2, H2) and a CO rich stream, o ff ering an added value, can be generated simultaneously to CO2 removal. The di ff erent composition ranges of a single stage and a two stage membrane process and the associated costs have been evaluated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The di ffi culty to achieve CO/N2 separation by membrane processes (but also by any process, be it cryogenics, absorption or adsorption) is a major limitation for pushing CO purity performances. Despite relatively low purity, high partial pressure CO can be produced in the retentate by membrane separation under feed compression. Such operation would produce two valuable streams, a high partial pressure CO product and a high purity CO2 product which could be both feed streams for carbon utilization processes. If CO2 separation cost is the main concern and/or if the CO stream must undergo further puri fi cation and thus a high partial pressure is not an asset, a vacuum operation allows a reduction of costs compared to the feed compres-
texts.text: The di ffi culty to achieve CO/N2 separation by membrane processes (but also by any process, be it cryogenics, absorption or adsorption) is a major limitation for pushing CO purity performances. Despite relatively low purity, high partial pressure CO can be produced in the retentate by membrane separation under feed compression. Such operation would produce two valuable streams, a high partial pressure CO product and a high purity CO2 product which could be both feed streams for carbon utilization processes.
